name: Data Structures and Algorithms
scope: A person with BASIC proficiency in Data Structures and Algorithms (1-2 years experience) should be able to recognise when a small product feature is using the wrong data structure or a brute-force algorithm and apply the correct one. They are fluent with arrays, hash maps, sets, stacks, queues, simple LRU caches (via an ordered map or hashmap+doubly-linked-list), sorting, and binary search on sorted (and locally monotonic) data. They can model a small problem as a directed or undirected graph, traverse it with BFS or DFS on an adjacency-list representation, detect cycles in a directed graph using DFS with three visit states (unvisited / in-progress / done), and identify connected components in an undirected graph. They can perform in / pre / post-order traversal on a binary tree, compute tree depth or height, find the lowest common ancestor of two nodes in a binary tree via a single post-order traversal, and search a BST. They can reason about Big-O for O(n), O(log n), O(1), and O(V + E) operations and write small performance tests that pin a complexity contract. They handle common edge cases (empty inputs, duplicates, boundary values, malformed data, disjoint graph components, same-node LCA queries, self-loops) and validate inputs at function boundaries with appropriate exceptions. They distinguish between using the right primitive (hash map vs list scan, stack vs counter, binary search vs linear scan, BFS/DFS vs nested loops, post-order LCA vs upward walk) and just making code work, and write idiomatic, readable code in their primary language. They write tests that cover correctness on small fixtures and add a basic performance check where the algorithm choice matters. They do NOT yet implement weighted shortest path (Dijkstra), dynamic programming, advanced trees (balanced, segment), tries beyond a toy example, heaps / priority queues for top-k, sliding-window with multiple invariants, backtracking, or NP-hard approximations.
long_scope:
{"sections": [{"name": "Arrays, Hashing, and Sets", "scope_included": "- Recognizes when a problem is best solved with O(n) one-pass scans plus a hash map or set
- Uses frequency counting, deduplication, two-sum / pair-finding patterns, and grouping by key
- Avoids accidental O(n^2) by replacing nested loops with a single hash-grouped pass when keys are stable
- Reasons about hash collisions and ordering guarantees in the chosen language's hash map
- Reads and writes idiomatic code that uses the language's native hash map and set types correctly", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Stacks and Queues", "scope_included": "- Uses a stack for matching/nesting problems (balanced brackets, simple undo-redo, expression parsing)
- Uses a FIFO queue/deque for level-order traversal and sliding-window patterns at a basic level
- Recognizes when a counter-based approach silently gives wrong answers vs a stack-based one
- Handles edge cases: empty input, stray closing tokens, alternating open/close at boundaries
- Avoids O(n) operations on the wrong end of a list (e.g. always uses deque/queue primitives, not list.pop(0))", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "LRU Cache (Hash Map + Linked List)", "scope_included": "- Understands why an LRU cache needs both a hash map (for O(1) lookup) and an ordered structure (for recency)
- Uses the language's ordered-map primitive (e.g. Python OrderedDict, Java LinkedHashMap) OR a hand-rolled hashmap+doubly-linked-list
- Maintains O(1) average get/put while moving touched keys to the most-recently-used end
- Distinguishes between recency (touched on read or write) and insertion order
- Pins eviction order with tests covering boundary cases (touch protects from eviction, put on existing key refreshes recency)", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Sorting and Binary Search", "scope_included": "- Recognizes when input is already sorted and a O(log n) lookup beats a linear scan
- Uses the language's binary-search helper (e.g. Python bisect, Java Collections.binarySearch, Go sort.Search) correctly, including boundary semantics (lower vs upper bound)
- Validates the assumption that input is sorted at function entry; raises a clear error if not
- Uses a stable sort by key for grouping or ranking patterns
- Reasons about the cost of sorting once vs repeatedly searching", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Strings and Simple Parsing", "scope_included": "- Performs prefix/suffix checks, simple parsing, and single-pass transformations idiomatically
- Recognizes when a problem is really a hash-counting problem in disguise
- Avoids unsafe lookahead patterns that crash on edge inputs
- Handles Unicode and empty-string inputs correctly", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Trivial Graph Algorithms on Adjacency Lists", "scope_included": "- Models a small problem as a directed or undirected graph and chooses an adjacency-list representation
- Traverses a graph with BFS (using a FIFO queue) or DFS (recursive or with an explicit stack)
- Detects cycles in a directed graph using DFS with three visit states (unvisited / in-progress / done) and recognises that a plain visited set misses diamond-DAG cases
- Identifies connected components in an undirected graph via repeated DFS / BFS from unvisited nodes
- Handles edge cases: self-loops, parallel edges, multiple disjoint components, empty edge list
- Reasons about O(V + E) traversal cost on sparse graphs (~10^3-10^4 nodes)", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Tree Traversal and Lowest Common Ancestor", "scope_included": "- Performs in-order, pre-order, and post-order traversal on a binary tree, both recursively and (for small trees) iteratively
- Computes tree depth and height with a single recursive pass
- Finds the lowest common ancestor (LCA) of two nodes in a binary tree via a single post-order traversal, without requiring parent pointers and without the O(h^2) walk-and-intersect approach
- Searches a binary search tree in O(h) time and handles the not-found case with a documented sentinel
- Handles edge cases: LCA where one node is an ancestor of the other, LCA queries for the same node, trees with a single node, deeply skewed trees (still within stack-depth limits)", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Big-O Reasoning and Performance Tests", "scope_included": "- Justifies why an implementation is O(n) / O(log n) / O(1) and how it differs from the previous version
- Picks input sizes for performance tests that distinguish the new bound from the old (e.g. 50K rows under 2s)
- Avoids over-claiming complexity (e.g. claiming O(1) when worst-case is O(n))
- Recognizes when a perf test is appropriate vs when correctness tests alone are sufficient", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Standard Library and Built-in Primitives", "scope_included": "- Knows which built-in collections fit which problem (hash map, ordered map, set, deque/queue, priority queue, sorted-search helper)
- Avoids reaching for third-party DSA libraries when standard library or built-ins suffice
- Reads documentation for unfamiliar stdlib helpers and integrates them correctly
- Understands the cost of each common built-in operation in the chosen language", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Edge Cases and Input Validation", "scope_included": "- Handles empty input, single-element input, and boundary values explicitly
- Validates assumptions at function entry (sorted input, non-null pointers, valid range) and raises clear errors
- Handles duplicates, overflow, and exact-boundary cases with intent (not by accident)
- Avoids silent corruption when an invariant is violated", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Tests for DSA Code", "scope_included": "- Writes correctness tests on small hand-built fixtures covering happy path and the known edge cases
- Adds a small performance test where the algorithmic choice is the point of the change
- Pins behaviour on regression cases reported by users (so the bug cannot silently come back)
- Keeps tests deterministic (no shared mutable state, seeded randomness when needed)", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Quality, Readability, and Working in a Codebase", "scope_included": "- Writes idiomatic code in the project's primary language with type hints/annotations where the language supports them
- Keeps function signatures stable when refactoring an internal implementation
- Avoids premature abstractions (does not introduce a new class hierarchy when a single function is enough)
- Adds the minimum surface area to the codebase (no unused helpers or dead code)", "is_non_core_section": true}, {"name": "Out-of-Scope (NOT expected at BASIC)", "scope_included": "- Weighted shortest path algorithms (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, A*)
- Dynamic programming (1D, 2D, or otherwise)
- Balanced trees, segment trees, suffix arrays / automata
- Tries beyond a single toy example
- Heaps / priority queues, top-k tracking
- Sliding-window patterns with multiple invariants
- Backtracking, branch-and-bound
- NP-hard approximations", "is_non_core_section": true}]}
